concepts.name: Classification of Alcohols â€“ Mono, Di, Tri, Polyhydric
concepts.explanation: Alcohols are classified as monohydric (one -OH), dihydric (two -OH), trihydric (three -OH), or polyhydric (many -OH) based on the number of hydroxyl groups. Examples: ethanol (mono), ethylene glycol (di), glycerol (tri).

concepts.name: Classification of Alcohols â€“ Primary, Secondary, Tertiary
concepts.explanation: Monohydric alcohols are classified by the nature of the carbon bearing the -OH group. Primary (1آ°): -OH attached to a carbon attached to one carbon. Secondary (2آ°): attached to a carbon attached to two carbons. Tertiary (3آ°): attached to a carbon attached to three carbons.

concepts.name: Allylic and Benzylic Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Allylic alcohols have the -OH attached to a spآ³ carbon adjacent to a C=C bond (e.g., CHâ‚‚=CHâ€“CHâ‚‚OH). Benzylic alcohols have the -OH attached to a spآ³ carbon adjacent to an aromatic ring (e.g., Câ‚†Hâ‚…â€“CHâ‚‚OH).

concepts.name: Classification of Phenols â€“ Mono, Di, Trihydric
concepts.explanation: Phenols are classified as monohydric (one -OH), dihydric (two -OH), or trihydric (three -OH) based on the number of hydroxyl groups attached to the aromatic ring. Examples: phenol, catechol (1,2-dihydroxybenzene), resorcinol (1,3-dihydroxybenzene), hydroquinone (1,4-dihydroxybenzene).

concepts.name: Classification of Ethers â€“ Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical
concepts.explanation: Ethers are classified as symmetrical (simple) if the two alkyl/aryl groups attached to oxygen are identical (e.g., Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OCâ‚‚Hâ‚…), and unsymmetrical (mixed) if the two groups are different (e.g., CHâ‚ƒOCâ‚‚Hâ‚…).

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: IUPAC names are derived from the parent alkane by replacing '-e' with '-ol'. The longest chain containing the -OH is numbered from the end nearer to the -OH. Examples: CHâ‚ƒOH (methanol), CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚OH (ethanol), CHâ‚ƒCH(OH)CHâ‚ƒ (propan-2-ol).

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Phenols
concepts.explanation: Phenol is the simplest hydroxybenzene. Substituted phenols use ortho, meta, para (common) or locants (IUPAC). Examples: o-cresol (2-methylphenol), m-cresol (3-methylphenol), p-cresol (4-methylphenol).

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Ethers
concepts.explanation: Common names: name the alkyl/aryl groups in alphabetical order followed by 'ether'. IUPAC: treat the larger group as the parent hydrocarbon and name the smaller group as an alkoxy substituent. Examples: CHâ‚ƒOCHâ‚ƒ (methoxymethane), Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OCâ‚‚Hâ‚… (ethoxyethane).

concepts.name: Structure of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
concepts.explanation: In alcohols and ethers, oxygen is spآ³ hybridised (approximately tetrahedral). The C-O-H bond angle in alcohols is slightly less than 109.5آ° (due to lone pair repulsion). In phenol, the C-O bond has partial double bond character (resonance), making it shorter than in alcohols.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alcohols â€“ Acid-Catalysed Hydration of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Alkenes react with water in presence of acid catalyst (Hâپ؛) to form alcohols. The reaction follows Markovnikov's rule. Example: CHâ‚ƒCH=CHâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O â†’ CHâ‚ƒCH(OH)CHâ‚ƒ (Hâپ؛).

concepts.name: Preparation of Alcohols â€“ Hydroboration-Oxidation
concepts.explanation: Alkenes react with diborane (BHâ‚ƒ) to form trialkylboranes, which are oxidised with Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚/OHâپ» to give alcohols. This proceeds with anti-Markovnikov addition. Example: CHâ‚ƒCH=CHâ‚‚ â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚CHâ‚‚OH (major).

concepts.name: Preparation of Alcohols â€“ Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes are reduced to primary alcohols; ketones are reduced to secondary alcohols. Reagents: Hâ‚‚/catalyst (Pd, Pt, Ni), NaBHâ‚„, or LiAlHâ‚„. Example: RCHO â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH; RCOR' â†’ RCH(OH)R'.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCHO + Hâ‚‚ â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH; RCOR' + Hâ‚‚ â†’ RCH(OH)R'

concepts.name: Preparation of Alcohols â€“ Reduction of Carboxylic Acids and Esters
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols by LiAlHâ‚„ or diborane (Bâ‚‚Hâ‚†). Esters are reduced to primary alcohols by LiAlHâ‚„ or catalytic hydrogenation. Example: RCOOH â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH (LiAlHâ‚„).
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOOH + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH; RCOOR' + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH + R'OH

concepts.name: Preparation of Alcohols â€“ From Grignard Reagents
concepts.explanation: Grignard reagents react with aldehydes and ketones to give alcohols after hydrolysis. Methanal gives primary alcohols, other aldehydes give secondary alcohols, and ketones give tertiary alcohols. Example: RMgX + R'CHO â†’ RCH(OH)R'.

concepts.name: Preparation of Phenols â€“ From Haloarenes
concepts.explanation: Chlorobenzene is fused with NaOH at 623 K and 320 atm to give sodium phenoxide, which on acidification gives phenol. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Cl + NaOH â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…ONa â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Cl + NaOH (623K, 320 atm) â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…ONa â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH (Hâپ؛)

concepts.name: Preparation of Phenols â€“ From Benzenesulphonic Acid
concepts.explanation: Benzene is sulphonated to benzenesulphonic acid, which is fused with NaOH to give sodium phenoxide, then acidified to phenol. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…SOâ‚ƒH + NaOH â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…ONa â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…SOâ‚ƒH + NaOH (melt) â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…ONa â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH (Hâپ؛)

concepts.name: Preparation of Phenols â€“ From Diazonium Salts
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts (from aniline) are hydrolysed by warming with water or dilute acid to give phenols. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH + Nâ‚‚ + HCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Xâپ» + Hâ‚‚O â†’ ArOH + Nâ‚‚ + HX (warm)

concepts.name: Preparation of Phenols â€“ From Cumene (Industrial Method)
concepts.explanation: Cumene (isopropylbenzene) is oxidised in air to cumene hydroperoxide, which is converted to phenol and acetone on treatment with dilute acid. This is the major industrial route for phenol.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CH(CHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…C(CHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚OOH â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH + CHâ‚ƒCOCHâ‚ƒ (Hâپ؛)

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alcohols and Phenols
concepts.explanation: Alcohols and phenols have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons and ethers of comparable molecular mass due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Boiling point increases with molecular mass and decreases with branching. Lower members are miscible with water due to H-bonding.

concepts.name: Acidity of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Alcohols are weak acids (pKa ~16-18). They react with active metals (Na, K) to form alkoxides and Hâ‚‚. The acidity decreases with increasing alkyl substitution: CHâ‚ƒOH > 1آ° > 2آ° > 3آ°. Alcohols are weaker acids than water.

concepts.name: Acidity of Phenols
concepts.explanation: Phenols are stronger acids than alcohols (pKa ~10) due to resonance stabilisation of the phenoxide ion. Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity; electron-donating groups decrease acidity. o- and p-Nitrophenols are much more acidic than phenol.

concepts.name: Esterification of Alcohols and Phenols (Acetylation)
concepts.explanation: Alcohols and phenols react with carboxylic acids, acid chlorides, or acid anhydrides to form esters. Acetylation introduces a CHâ‚ƒCO group. Example: ROH + CHâ‚ƒCOCl â†’ ROCOCHâ‚ƒ + HCl (pyridine).

concepts.name: Reaction with Hydrogen Halides â€“ Lucas Test
concepts.explanation: Alcohols react with HX to form haloalkanes. Lucas reagent (conc. HCl + ZnClâ‚‚) distinguishes 1آ°, 2آ°, and 3آ° alcohols: 3آ° gives immediate turbidity, 2آ° in 5-10 minutes, 1آ° at room temperature does not react. Reactivity: 3آ° > 2آ° > 1آ°.

concepts.name: Dehydration of Alcohols to Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Alcohols undergo acid-catalysed dehydration to form alkenes. Reagents: conc. Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„, Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚„, or Alâ‚‚Oâ‚ƒ. Ease of dehydration: 3آ° > 2آ° > 1آ°. Example: Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OH â†’ CHâ‚‚=CHâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O (conc. Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„, 443K).

concepts.name: Mechanism of Acid-Catalysed Dehydration of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: The mechanism involves: (1) protonation of the -OH group; (2) formation of a carbocation (rate-determining step); (3) elimination of a proton to form the alkene. The ease follows carbocation stability (3آ° > 2آ° > 1آ°).

concepts.name: Oxidation of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Primary alcohols are oxidised to aldehydes (mild oxidants like PCC) or carboxylic acids (strong oxidants like KMnOâ‚„, Kâ‚‚Crâ‚‚Oâ‚‡). Secondary alcohols are oxidised to ketones (CrOâ‚ƒ). Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCHâ‚‚OH â†’ RCHO (PCC) â†’ RCOOH (KMnOâ‚„); Râ‚‚CHOH â†’ Râ‚‚CO (CrOâ‚ƒ)

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Phenols â€“ Nitration
concepts.explanation: Phenols undergo nitration with dilute HNOâ‚ƒ to give o- and p-nitrophenols. o-Nitrophenol is steam volatile due to intramolecular H-bonding; p-nitrophenol is less volatile. With conc. HNOâ‚ƒ, picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) is formed.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Phenols â€“ Halogenation
concepts.explanation: Phenol reacts with bromine in CSâ‚‚ or CHClâ‚ƒ at low temperature to give o- and p-bromophenols. With bromine water, phenol gives 2,4,6-tribromophenol (white precipitate).

concepts.name: Kolbe's Reaction
concepts.explanation: Phenoxide ion (from phenol + NaOH) reacts with COâ‚‚ at high pressure to give o-hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid) after acidification. The ortho product is favoured due to chelation.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH + NaOH â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…ONa + COâ‚‚ â†’ o-HOCâ‚†Hâ‚„COOH (Hâپ؛)

concepts.name: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction
concepts.explanation: Phenol reacts with CHClâ‚ƒ in presence of NaOH to give o-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde). The intermediate dichlorocarbene (:CClâ‚‚) is generated and attacks the phenoxide ion ortho to the -OH group.

concepts.name: Reaction of Phenol with Zinc Dust
concepts.explanation: Phenol on heating with zinc dust gives benzene. The oxygen is removed as ZnO. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH + Zn â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚† + ZnO.

concepts.name: Oxidation of Phenol to Benzoquinone
concepts.explanation: Phenol on oxidation with chromic acid gives p-benzoquinone (a conjugated diketone). Phenols are slowly oxidised by air to dark coloured mixtures.

concepts.name: Methanol â€“ Preparation and Uses
concepts.explanation: Methanol (wood spirit) is prepared by catalytic hydrogenation of CO with Hâ‚‚ (ZnO-Crâ‚‚Oâ‚ƒ catalyst, high pressure). It is a colourless liquid, highly poisonous (causes blindness). Used as a solvent and for making formaldehyde.

concepts.name: Ethanol â€“ Fermentation and Uses
concepts.explanation: Ethanol is obtained by fermentation of sugars (glucose/fructose) using enzymes (invertase converts sucrose, zymase converts glucose to ethanol). It is a colourless liquid, used as a solvent and beverage. Denatured alcohol is made unfit for drinking.

concepts.name: Preparation of Ethers â€“ Dehydration of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Primary alcohols undergo acid-catalysed dehydration to give ethers (at lower temperature). Example: 2Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OH â†’ Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OCâ‚‚Hâ‚… + Hâ‚‚O (conc. Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„, 413K). This method is suitable only for primary alcohols; secondary/tertiary give alkenes.

concepts.name: Preparation of Ethers â€“ Williamson Synthesis
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides react with sodium alkoxides (or phenoxides) to form ethers (Sâ‚™2). Example: R-X + R'ONa â†’ R-O-R' + NaX. Primary alkyl halides give the best yields; tertiary halides undergo elimination.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Ethers
concepts.explanation: Ethers have boiling points comparable to alkanes of similar molecular mass (much lower than alcohols) due to absence of H-bonding. They are moderately soluble in water (oxygen forms H-bonds with water).

concepts.name: Cleavage of Ethers by Hydrogen Halides
concepts.explanation: Ethers are cleaved by concentrated HX (HI > HBr > HCl) at high temperature to give alkyl halides and alcohols. The reaction proceeds via protonation followed by nucleophilic attack. Example: ROR' + HI â†’ RI + R'OH.
concepts.formula_reaction: ROR' + HX â†’ RX + R'OH (excess HX gives 2RX)

concepts.name: Mechanism of Ether Cleavage
concepts.explanation: Step 1: Protonation of ether oxygen. Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of Iâپ» (or Brâپ») on the less hindered carbon (Sâ‚™2) or formation of carbocation (Sâ‚™1 for tertiary groups). Mixed ethers cleave at the bond forming the more stable alkyl halide.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Anisole (Phenyl Methyl Ether)
concepts.explanation: Anisole (Câ‚†Hâ‚…OCHâ‚ƒ) undergoes electrophilic substitution at ortho and para positions. The -OCHâ‚ƒ group activates the ring via resonance. Examples: bromination gives p-bromoanisole; nitration gives o- and p-nitroanisole; Friedel-Crafts reactions give o- and p-products.
